items.title: Tree climbing Part 2
items.audioPath: 【BBC】08-23年+bbc+6分钟英语/2010 6分钟英语/100303 Tree climbing Part 2/100311_6min_treeclimbing2_for_web_au_bb.mp3
items.transcript: This is 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com. Hello and welcome to this week's 6 Minute English. I'm Dan Walkersmith and today, for the second part of our tree climbing programme, I'm joined by Kate. OK, Kate, well, last week we were talking about tree climbing and we covered some interesting vocabulary including the different parts of the tree. So I was wondering if you could quickly remind us of some of those. Of course. The trunk is the main body of the tree, the branches are the tree's arms and the leaves are the flat green parts at the end of the branches. Later on in the show, we're going to be talking about the world's tallest tree. But this week's question for you, Kate, is where would you find the world's oldest tree? Is it A, in Australia, B, in Tanzania or C, in Sweden? Oh, that's a really difficult question. I have no idea but I'm going to guess and go for A, Australia. We'll see if you're right at the end of the programme. Now, let's hear about the world's tallest tree. Here's tree expert Tony Russell to tell us all about it. Have a listen to the extract. How tall is the tree? The biggest giant redwood is in California and it's one called the General Sherman tree and it is an astonishing size. In height, it's about 275 feet tall. Its circumference, the ground level, is over 100 feet with a diameter across of 36 feet. Astonishing thing. So the tree is around 275. 275 feet tall. As Tony said, it's an astonishing size. What does astonishing mean here, Kate? Well, astonishing can mean both surprising and amazing. If something's astonishing, it's basically so incredible that you almost can't believe it. And as well as the height, Tony described the circumference and the diameter of the tree. Now, what do these words mean, Kate? Well, they're both words that relate to a tree's size. Circumference, is the distance around a circle or around shape. And diameter would be the distance across a circle. So in this case, across the width of the tree. Can you imagine being at the top of that, Kate? Oh, I don't think I'd want to be, actually. I'm not very good with heights. Well, some people spend practically their whole lives climbing trees. Last week, for example, we heard from the professional tree climber, James Aldred. He was talking about how he started climbing. So let's hear him again as he goes up a tree. We join him just as he's preparing to camp for the night, just above the top of the tree. What does he mean there, Kate? OK, well, to camp means to sleep outdoors, usually in a tent or under some sort of temporary shelter. And the canopy here is the upper layer. So in this case, it's the forest's roof. But it really means anything that spreads out above you. Let's have a listen to James as he prepares to camp. Can you tell me what he's going to sleep in instead of a bed? So this is where I'm going to camp for the night. The sun is just about to dip beneath the canopy level. I'm just above the canopy, the surrounding canopy. So if I rig my hammock here, I should catch the last rays of sun. Right, so James is setting up a hammock to sleep in. A hammock is a swinging bed made of cloth or rope. Let's have a listen to the final extract. Here's James at the top of the tree. He says it's a hell of a view from up there. Can you explain this, Kate? Well, saying that it's a hell of a view is an informal way of saying that the view is incredible. The phrase hell of a... ...emphasises any quality that you're talking about, whether it's good or bad. And here it's pretty obvious that James thinks the view is amazing. OK, well, let's have a listen to the extract. How does he describe the area around him? It is a hell of a view from here. Just looking out over endless forest. I can hear cars, I can hear traffic, but I can't see them. For all intents and purposes, I'm in the middle of wilderness. So he says that for all intents and purposes, he's in the middle of... ...the middle of wilderness. Kate, what's he saying here? OK, well, wilderness means an area of land that's never been developed by man. It's still wild, so it's called the wilderness. And when he says for all intents and purposes? This phrase, for all intents and purposes, means in a practical sense. So James isn't actually in the wilderness, but because he can't see any cars or buildings, for all intents and purposes, it appears like he is. Well, we're almost out of time. So let's go over some of the vocabulary we've come across today. First of all, we had astonishing, circumference, diameter, to camp, canopy, hammock, helluva, for all intents and purposes, and wilderness. And today's question was, Kate, in which country would you find the world's oldest living tree? And I took a complete guess and said Australia. I was quite surprised by this. The answer is, in fact, Sweden. Wow, that is surprising. You wouldn't expect it to be in Sweden. It doesn't strike me as the kind of place to have that sort of wilderness about it. The tree itself is a Norway spruce called Old Tjiko, and apparently it's 9,550 years old. Wow, that's so old. In fact, this tree is almost 5,000 years older than the second oldest recorded tree, which can be found in California. But Kate, could you tell us about the largest tree in the world? Well, the largest tree by volume, including all its trunk, branches and leaves, is called El Tule and can be found in Mexico. It has a circumference of 119 feet, so it's 36 metres around the base, and its diameter is 38 feet, which is almost 12 metres across. I've actually been to that tree and it is massive, absolutely huge. Oh, I can hardly even imagine it. It's odd because it's not very tall. It just goes on and on. It's the size of a house, basically. It's absolutely massive. Wow. But I'm afraid that's all we have time for here this week, so thanks very much for listening and goodbye. Goodbye. That was 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com.

items.title: Top international universities
items.audioPath: 【BBC】08-23年+bbc+6分钟英语/2010 6分钟英语/100923 Top international universities/100923_university_for_web_au_bb.mp3
items.transcript: This is 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com. Hello, I'm Rob and this is 6 Minute English. I'm joined today by Alice. Hi Alice. Hi Rob. Today we're talking about universities around the world and a new list that shows the top 200. I'm going to start by asking you a question, Alice. Which university has come number one in the list? Hmm, there are so many famous universities around the world. Let me guess, is it Cambridge? Well, Cambridge University in England is certainly on the list, but you'll have to wait until the end of the programme to see if you're right. OK. This new list is called the London Times Higher Education List. It ranks universities on a number of factors. These include its quality of teaching, the influence of its research and the income it gets for research. So, what's the list? So, if these factors are higher, the university gets a higher ranking. That's right. Of course, funding is very important. Funding. That's money in other words. Is a university more successful if it gets more funding? Well, that's what Professor Steve Smith thinks. He's president of Universities UK, which represents all British universities. He says the US puts two and a half times the amount of gross domestic product, or GDP, into the UK's gross domestic product. The US puts two and a half times the amount of GDP into its universities than we do, and therefore, surprisingly, they get two and a half times the number of universities in the top 200. I think this is all about the investment that countries put in the future, all about the investment they put in their universities. Professor Steve Smith says it's all about the investment the country puts into their universities that makes them successful. OK. So he says... Can I change my answer now? No, Alice. You may still be right. Fourteen British institutions are on the list, including Oxford and Cambridge. It's interesting that the institutions which dominate the top of the list are English-speaking ones, as BBC reporter Johnny Hogg explains. It's English-speaking universities, and particularly those in America, that dominate the top spots in this list. You have to go down to number 15 to find the first non-Anglophone establishment, the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology. And surprisingly, France's renowned École Polytechnique only comes in 39th. Ah, so American universities dominate the top of the list. But what does he mean about the first non-Anglophone establishment being at number 15? Well, Anglophone means English-speaking. So the first non-Anglophone university in the list was... Was... The Swiss Federal Institute of Technology. But come on, Rob, who was number one? Wait and see, Alice. Let's find out about some of the other countries in the list. Here's Johnny Hogg again. Elsewhere, Asia has done well, with Hong Kong, Japan, South Korea and China all having universities in the top 40. Only two African institutions, one the University of Cape Town in South Africa, the other the University of Alexandria in Egypt, make the top two. So Johnny Hogg said that universities in Hong Kong, Japan, South Korea and China were in the top 40. Yes, mainland China has six universities on the list, and there are two in Africa. OK, here's another question for you. When you choose a university, does it really matter if it has a good ranking? Hmm, the reasons for choosing a university. I suppose its reputation is important. Well, of course. Although the authors of this new list say... That reputation is not as important as it used to be. I chose my university because it offered the subject I wanted to do. Really? I chose mine because it was close to home and it had a lively social scene. A good reason. Let's hear the reasons why Kate and Kaz chose their places to study. Well, I went to Edinburgh University and I chose Edinburgh because I love the city. The university had a very good reputation and it was close enough to my home town. It was close enough to my home town without actually being my home town, so it was only about an hour away, so I could go home at weekends if I wanted to. I went to Sussex University in the 1970s and I chose Sussex above other universities because it had an excellent reputation in biology, which was the subject I studied. So Kate is like me. She chose somewhere that was not too far from home. She could go home at weekends. And the university in Edinburgh had a good reputation. And what about Kaz? For him, it had an excellent reputation for teaching biology, so it seems reputation does actually count. OK Alice, it's time to tell you what was the top university in the London Times Higher Education list. You thought it was… Cambridge, am I right? Well, Cambridge and Oxford in the UK are in the top ten, but the number one university is Harvard in the USA. Of course. Of course. That's actually my second choice. I think you need to go back to university to do some more learning. But before you do, Alice, could you tell us some of the words that we've learnt today? Research Income Ranking Funding Gross domestic product Investment Institutions Anglophone Reputation That's all we've got time for today. Thanks for joining us. And see you next time. Bye. That was 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com.

items.title: Invisible island
items.transcript: This is 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com. Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. We'll be talking about a story in the news and learning some vocabulary along the way. I'm Alice and joining me today is Neil. Welcome, Neil. Hi there, Alice. Now, Neil, are you good with maps? Good with maps? You mean, am I good at reading maps? Yes. Can you find your way to a place you want to go to just by looking at a map? Well, actually, I need a map because I used to think I had a great sense of direction, but now I have to admit I've got a terrible sense of direction. I almost always walk in the wrong direction automatically, so I need a map. You need a map. Oh, well, I like to think I'm a fairly good navigator. That's someone who finds or plots the way. But what if the map is wrong? I once walked around a town for a whole hour using a map I'd downloaded from the internet, and then I realised the map I was using was completely wrong. Oh dear, it does happen. Yes, it does. And in 6 Minute English today, we'll be hearing about an island that has appeared on maps for several years, but which people now realise has never existed at all. Aha, this is the famous Sandy Island. Australia. Yes, it's all very suspicious. And Neil, as we're talking about islands, my question for you today is about a legendary island which is supposed to have sunk into the ocean thousands of years ago. Was it called... Well, I will have a think about that and tell you at the end of the programme. OK. As usual, we find out... I don't know what the answer is at the end. Now, let's hear more about Sandy Island, the island that never was. Maria Seaton from the University of Sydney in Australia was on an expedition in the sea between Australia and New Caledonia. We were actually out in the Eastern Coral Sea conducting a scientific research expedition. And when we were approaching the area of this supposed island, we saw that our scientific map showed there was an island there. And yet the navigation charts on board the vessel showed that we had a water depth of 1,400 metres. So that's where we started getting suspicious. Maria Seaton and her research team were looking at their scientific maps which showed an island. But they became suspicious. They questioned what they saw. Why? Because when they got to the area where the ship was supposed to be, the navigation charts on board the vessel, the ship, showed that all that there was... was 1,400 metres of water. So the island wasn't there. Could it have sunk? Here's BBC correspondent Duncan Kennedy in Australia. It hasn't sunk. No, it was never there. The Australian Naval Maps Department, the department that makes naval maps, said it could simply be human error just repeated down the years. Did you hear what the department that makes naval maps in Australia said about Sandy Island appearing on its maps? They said... It could be human error repeated through the years. That means one person made a mistake and put the island on the map and then other people just copied their map over and over again. Interesting. But how could all maps make the same mistake? The island appeared on standard maps, nautical maps, that's maps of the ocean, and even Google Earth which is largely made up of photos from space. Well, people who make maps use a variety of sources. They say lots of different reference materials. So maybe it wasn't worth taking thousands of photos of the sea, but easier to copy someone else's map. Here's what the BBC's Duncan Kennedy says about the mistake. Sandy Island appears on standard maps, it appears on nautical maps, and it even appears on Google Earth. And a spokesman for Google Earth Maps Australia said they use a variety of sources to compile their maps, but that things change. And they say... They're advising people to tell them if they spot similar mistakes, could they let them know? The BBC's Duncan Kennedy, who says mapmakers like Google Earth have advised people to tell them if they spot similar mistakes on any of their maps. Yes, things change. Even islands disappear sometimes. Which brings me to the answer to your question at the beginning of 6 Minute English, Alice. I think I know the name of that legendary island you were talking about. Ah, yes. I asked you if it was called A, Pacifica, B, Atlantis, or C, Oceania. And I thought it was a trick question, but I'm going to go with what I first thought, which is Atlantis. And you're right. Atlantis was the name given to a legendary island which was supposed to have been somewhere between Africa and Europe before it sunk. It was talked about by Plato in the 4th century BC. And Neil, I hope you'll share some of the words we've heard in today's programme. Yes, of course. Here they are. Thanks so much, Neil. And please join us again soon for more 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com. And don't forget you can find us on Facebook and Twitter. We really do exist there, honestly. Bye for now. Bye. From bbclearningenglish.com.

items.title: 6min bus technology problems audio
items.transcript: This is 6 Minute English from bbclearningenglish.com. Hello and welcome to another episode of 6 Minute English with me, Feifei. And me, Neil. How are you, Neil, and what have you been up to? Oh, I'm annoyed. I was doing all this work on the scripts for this programme on my smartphone and it crashed. Because it's my phone, I can't get IT support at work to sort it out for me. Serves you right for using your own device. Yeah, I'm not alone though. A survey by a business technology company called Avanade has found that 88% of executives said employees were using their own personal computing technologies for business purposes. So you're saying lots of people are using their own smartphones and laptops instead of the company computers? Yeah, more or less. Either instead of company technology or as well as the company technology. And in the name of further research, I went out, and did my own very unscientific survey. When we have a problem with our laptops, we would normally call just sort of IT support really. So we can call them at any time, but they don't always sort out the problems. So that in a way create a problem, especially when we try to catch deadlines. Sometimes I find hard to speak with the IT department, because it seems to me that they speak one language and the rest of us speak a different one. They use too many technical terms. So I sometimes find it very frustrating, the dealing with technology. I constantly come across problems with software update, such as Flash Player, which is required to play video and audio. But you need to have the most updated version of Flash Player, which we don't always have. Yeah. So that's the problem. It can be very frustrating if you don't have it. And then you ask for IT support, but sometimes they don't know what you are talking about. So it's not always easy to get everything up and running, even if it's the office computer. No, as I'm sure you know, Feifei, there are always glitches or problems. And that's when you need to be able to talk to your company's IT support, that's information technology, to get things up and running again. But what should you say? Ah, look, it's Business Betty. Hi, Betty. Great to have you here, as usual. Thank you, Neil. Love the hat. Thank you, Neil. Always good to be here. Now, whatever device you're using and wherever you are, when you have a technological problem, you need to be able to explain what the problem is. And you need to be able to understand what the IT expert is saying to you. Let's start with the problems. Maybe you can't connect to the internet. I can't connect to the internet. Maybe you can't print anything. I can't print anything. Maybe you saved some files to your hard drive and they've disappeared, Feifei. I saved some files to my hard drive and they have disappeared. So, the IT support person might ask you a few questions. Has this happened before? Has this happened before? What operating system are you running? What operating system are you running? What version of the software are you using? What version of the software are you using? He or she might offer you some advice. For example, you need to install an update. You need to install an update. Try reinstalling the programme. Try reinstalling the programme. Or the problem might be more difficult to fix. For example, they might say, your system needs a rebuild. Your system needs a rebuild. Or maybe it can't be fixed at all. I'm afraid this can't be fixed. Hopefully it won't ever be that bad. And of course, they might give the advice most of us have heard at one time and which seems to work very well. Give your computer a reboot. Turn it off and on again. Give your computer a reboot. Turn it off and on again. So, there you have everything you need to keep the technology in your life working well. Brilliant, Betty. Thanks. My pleasure. So, if there's nothing else, I'll be off. Bye for now. Bye, Betty. Ah, that's our Betty. She knows all about IT issues, you know. She is a legend. Do you feel a technology roleplay coming on? Yep. I'm the boss, right? Yep. There isn't a boss in this one. But you can be the IT support worker if you like. OK. All right. So, you have an IT problem and you're asking me for help, right? Yes. Are you ready? I'm ready. Hello, IT support. Can I help you? Hello. Yes, I've got a problem. I saved some files to my hard drive and they just have disappeared. Ah. Has this happened before? Yes, a couple of times. If it's happened a couple of times, you might need to install an update. And if that doesn't work? Well, if that doesn't work, your system needs a rebuild. Try installing the update and let me know how it goes. OK. Thanks for your help. You're welcome. Roleplay. Roleplay. And that's the end of today's roleplay. How was it for you? It was great, Neil. You sounded like you actually knew what you were talking about. Well, that's because I'm brilliant. You can call me any time you have a problem, Feifei. But you're not really an IT expert, are you? You can't even fix your own smartphone. Ah. Don't joke, Feifei. I've lost the whole of the series. She's going to have to write them again. Good luck to you for the next time you're dealing with any IT issues. Yes, it's good luck from me too and goodbye from both of us. Join us again soon for another episode of 6 Minute English. Bye.
